facts_disclosed: Switched to Kuapa Kokoo, 960 cedis/bag + 75 cedis fair trade, 131/147 voted yes

turns.content: good news my friend. the cooperative has made a big change. we voted to switch our buying agent
That sounds like a major decision for the cooperative! Switching buying agents takes a lot of coordination. What happened, and who are you moving to?
we have moved to Kuapa Kokoo. the old agent Akuafo failed us. during last harvest they did not come to collect for 3 weeks. 3 weeks my friend
Three weeks without collection during harvest is a serious failure. That's the critical window when beans need to move quickly. What happened to the cocoa that was waiting?
we lost bags to mould. sitting in the shed for 3 weeks even with good drying the moisture comes back. some families lost 2 or 3 bags each. that is money gone. that cannot happen again

That's devastating -- mould losses after all the work of growing, harvesting, fermenting, and drying. Each lost bag represents months of effort. I can see why the cooperative decided to make a change.

Kuapa Kokoo is one of the largest licensed buying companies in Ghana. They have a strong reputation. What terms did you negotiate with them?
the price is 960 cedis per bag of 64kg. and there is a fair trade premium of 75 cedis on top. so each bag brings 1,035 cedis total

960 plus 75 cedis fair trade premium is a strong price. That 1,035 per bag is a significant improvement. The fair trade premium is one of the real advantages of working with Kuapa Kokoo -- it's money that goes directly to farming communities.

How does the cooperative plan to use the premium? Does it go to individual families or into a shared fund?
we agreed to split it. part to the families and part to a cooperative community fund. for projects that help everyone. the elders said this is fair
That's a balanced approach -- families see direct benefit and the community builds resources for shared needs. How was the voting? Did the cooperative reach a clear consensus?
very clear. 147 families voted. 131 said yes to Kuapa Kokoo. only 16 voted no. the elders agreed too. it was a strong decision my friend

131 out of 147 is nearly 90% -- that's an overwhelming mandate. When that many families and the elders all agree, it shows the decision was clearly the right one for the community.

Did you lead the negotiations with Kuapa Kokoo?
yes. I went with 3 elders to their office in Kumasi. we brought our quality records from 3 seasons. they saw our beans are grade 1 most years. that gave us strength in the negotiation

Coming prepared with 3 seasons of quality data was a smart move. Grade 1 beans consistently -- that's exactly what a buyer wants to see. It proves your cooperative is reliable and delivers quality product.

Kuapa Kokoo knows they're getting premium cocoa, so they're willing to offer premium terms. What about collection logistics -- did you set a schedule?
every 10 days minimum during harvest season. we insisted on that. it is written in the agreement. after what Akuafo did nobody wants to risk cocoa sitting in the shed again

Every 10 days written into the agreement is excellent. That gives you something concrete to hold them to. If they miss a collection window, you have it in writing.

A few things to keep track of going forward:
- Note the date of every collection in a logbook
- Record the number of bags and the grade at each pickup
- Keep copies of all receipts

If there's ever a dispute, those records protect the cooperative.
the treasurer keeps a ledger. every bag in, every bag out, every cedi received. we learned from the Akuafo situation. trust must be verified my friend

That's the right approach. Trust but verify -- especially when the livelihoods of 147 families depend on it.

How long is the agreement with Kuapa Kokoo?

2 years to start. then we review and extend if both sides are satisfied. 'when the path is good you walk it further.' but if they fail like Akuafo we move again. the cooperative will not be held hostage

A 2-year agreement with a review is the right structure. It gives Kuapa Kokoo enough time to build a relationship with the cooperative, but keeps the door open if things go wrong.

This is a well-negotiated deal all around: better price, fair trade premium, reliable collection schedule, and a clear exit path. The 131 families who voted yes made a strong choice. Congratulations on leading this.
medaase. it was the cooperative's decision not mine alone. 'when the whole village carries the load, nobody bends.' Nyame willing the first harvest with Kuapa Kokoo will go well

A good proverb and true. The strength of 131 families speaking together is what made this deal possible. Nyame willing, the first harvest will prove the decision right.

Good luck with the new season. Take care my friend.
